questions.stem: How does Python code get executed on a CPU?
options: Python source code is directly run by the CPU as machine code
Python source → compiled to bytecode (.pyc) → CPython VM interprets bytecode → machine instructions
Python is always compiled to C first, then compiled to machine code
Python code runs in a JavaScript engine inside the browser
questions.explanation: Python's execution pipeline: (1) CPython compiles source.py to bytecode (platform-independent .pyc files), (2) the CPython virtual machine (VM) interprets each bytecode instruction one by one, (3) each bytecode instruction ultimately calls C code that produces CPU machine instructions. This interpretation overhead is why pure Python is ~50-100× slower than compiled languages for numeric loops.

questions.stem: What is the GIL (Global Interpreter Lock) and what does it prevent?
options: A lock on file access — prevents two programs writing the same file
A lock in CPython that only allows one thread to execute Python bytecode at a time — prevents true CPU parallelism with Python threads
A graphics library lock — prevents multiple threads from drawing to screen
A lock that prevents importing the same module twice
questions.explanation: The GIL is a mutex in CPython's C code that must be held to execute any Python bytecode. Even with 8 CPU threads, only one runs Python at a time. This means Python threads cannot parallelise CPU-bound code. The GIL IS released for I/O and for C extensions (NumPy, PyTorch) — so numpy operations and GPU kernels DO run in parallel. For true CPU parallelism: use multiprocessing instead of threading.

questions.stem: Why is NumPy's a + b vastly faster than [x + y for x, y in zip(a, b)] for large arrays?
options: NumPy uses a different mathematical definition of addition
NumPy calls optimised C code with SIMD instructions on contiguous memory, eliminating Python interpreter overhead for every element — one C call vs millions of Python bytecode dispatches
NumPy caches all previous addition results
Python list comprehensions have a bug that makes them artificially slow
questions.explanation: A Python loop has overhead per iteration: bytecode dispatch, type lookup, reference counting. For a million elements, that's millions of operations. NumPy's a + b makes one call into C code, which uses SIMD to process 8-16 elements per CPU instruction. The ratio is roughly: 1 C function call vs 1,000,000 Python bytecode dispatches — explaining the 50-100× speedup.

questions.stem: What does torch.compile() do, and when does the speedup occur?
options: It converts Python code to TensorFlow format
On the FIRST call: traces the computation graph and compiles to optimised machine code (Triton CUDA kernels). SUBSEQUENT calls execute the compiled native code — 2-5× faster
It makes the model smaller by removing unused layers
It converts the model to run on CPU instead of GPU
questions.explanation: torch.compile() uses PyTorch's Dynamo + Inductor backend: Dynamo traces Python execution to capture the computation graph; Inductor generates optimised Triton CUDA kernels with operator fusion (e.g. linear + activation → one kernel instead of two). The first call is slow (~10s for a large model). All subsequent calls use the compiled path — avoiding Python interpreter overhead for every operation.

questions.stem: What is the difference between compiled and interpreted languages?
options: Compiled languages require a GPU; interpreted languages run on CPU
Compiled languages translate source code to machine code before execution (C, C++, Rust); interpreted languages translate and execute line by line at runtime (Python, JavaScript)
Compiled languages are always faster — there is no trade-off
Interpreted languages cannot use loops or functions
questions.explanation: Compiled languages (C, C++, Rust, Go) translate all code to machine instructions before running — fast execution, no runtime translation overhead. Interpreted languages (Python, Ruby) translate on-the-fly — slower for computation but faster to write, debug, and iterate. Python's success in AI comes from combining Python's ease of use with C/CUDA extensions (NumPy, PyTorch) for the hot numerical paths.

questions.stem: What does cProfile.run('my_function()') tell you, and why should you always profile before optimising?
options: It checks if the function has any syntax errors
It measures time spent in each function call — showing where actual bottlenecks are, which is often NOT where you expect them to be
It converts the function to run on GPU automatically
It tests the function with different inputs to find bugs
questions.explanation: cProfile records how many times each function was called and how much cumulative time was spent in it. Engineers consistently misjudge where bottlenecks are — often spending hours optimising a function that accounts for 2% of runtime while the real bottleneck (data loading, string processing, I/O) is ignored. Profile first, then optimise only the actual hotspot. In ML code, the bottleneck is usually data loading, not the model forward pass.
